title: The Power of Dhikr: What It Is, Why It Transforms You, and the Best Phrases to Practice
excerpt: Dhikr — the remembrance of Allah — is not a ritual. It is a state of heart. This guide covers the Quranic and hadith evidence for dhikr, the most powerful phrases and their exact rewards, and how to build a dhikr practice into your daily life.
author: QuranBookk
content:
Allah says in the Quran:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ"
  translation="Verily, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest."
  reference="Surah Ar-Ra'd 13:28"
/>

This verse is not a metaphor. It is a law of the human heart — as precise and reliable as any law of physics. The heart that is disconnected from the remembrance of Allah will not find lasting peace in anything else: not wealth, not relationships, not achievement. The heart that remembers Allah will find a quality of stillness that nothing in this world can produce or destroy.

## What Is Dhikr?

Dhikr (ذِكْر) means remembrance, mention, or consciousness of Allah. It encompasses:

- **Verbal dhikr** — spoken phrases glorifying Allah (tasbih, tahmid, takbir, tahlil, istighfar, and others)
- **Dhikr of the heart** — inner awareness of Allah's presence, attributes, and blessings
- **Dhikr through action** — performing any act with the consciousness that Allah sees it

The highest form integrates all three: the tongue speaks, the heart is present, and the actions align with that awareness.

## The Quranic Command

Allah does not merely recommend dhikr — He commands it abundantly:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا وَسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا"
  translation="O you who have believed, remember Allah with much remembrance and exalt Him morning and evening."
  reference="Surah Al-Ahzab 33:41–42"
/>

The phrase *dhikran kathiran* (much remembrance) indicates no fixed number — the command is to remember Allah abundantly, consistently, throughout the day.

## The Most Powerful Dhikr Phrases

### 1. Subhanallah (سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ) — Glory be to Allah

<HadithBlock
  text="Two words are beloved to the Most Merciful, light on the tongue, heavy on the scale: Subhan Allahi wa bihamdihi, Subhan Allahil 'Adhim."
  narrator="Abu Hurairah (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih al-Bukhari 6406, Sahih Muslim 2694"
/>

*Subhan Allahi wa bihamdihi* — "Glory be to Allah and praise be to Him"
*Subhan Allahil 'Adhim* — "Glory be to Allah the Magnificent"

These are the two most beloved phrases to Allah in terms of weight on the Scales, despite being effortless to say.

### 2. Alhamdulillah (الحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ) — All Praise to Allah

<HadithBlock
  text="The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Al-hamdulillah fills the scale.'"
  narrator="Abu Malik al-Ash'ari (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih Muslim 223"
/>

Gratitude expressed as *alhamdulillah* — "all praise and gratitude belong to Allah" — is one of the most powerful acts of worship. It acknowledges that every blessing is from Allah and actively combats the human tendency toward ingratitude.

### 3. Allahu Akbar (اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ) — Allah Is the Greatest

*Allahu Akbar* is the takbir — the declaration that Allah is greater than everything. Greater than your fear, your problem, your deadline, your diagnosis, your heartbreak. Repeating it consciously in moments of difficulty recalibrates the heart's perspective instantly.

### 4. La ilaha illallah (لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ) — There Is No God but Allah

<HadithBlock
  text="The best dhikr is 'La ilaha illallah' and the best supplication is 'Alhamdulillah.'"
  narrator="Jabir ibn Abdullah (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sunan al-Tirmidhi 3383 — Authenticated by al-Albani"
/>

The tahlil is the most comprehensive dhikr — it is the essence of Islamic monotheism compressed into four words. Reciting it 100 times in the morning carries the reward of freeing ten slaves, earns 100 good deeds, erases 100 bad deeds, and protects against shaytan until the evening. (Sahih al-Bukhari 3293)

### 5. Astaghfirullah (أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللَّهَ) — I Seek Allah's Forgiveness

The Prophet ﷺ — despite being sinless — sought forgiveness more than 70 times per day. He said: *"Turn to Allah in repentance, for I turn to Him in repentance one hundred times a day."* (Sahih Muslim 2702)

Istighfar is not only for sins. It is a recognition of one's utter dependence on Allah's mercy and an acknowledgement that even one's acts of worship fall short of His deserving.

### 6. La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah (لَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ)

The Prophet ﷺ described this as a treasure from the treasures of Paradise. It means: "There is no movement or power except through Allah." In moments of helplessness — when you face what you cannot control — this phrase is the Muslim's anchor.

## The Tasbih After Prayer

After every obligatory prayer, the Prophet ﷺ taught:

- **33 times**: Subhan Allah
- **33 times**: Alhamdulillah
- **33 times**: Allahu Akbar
- **Once**: La ilaha illallahu wahdahu la sharika lahu, lahul mulku wa lahul hamdu wa huwa 'ala kulli shay'in qadir

This equals 100 phrases. The Prophet ﷺ said: *"Whoever does this after every prayer, his sins will be forgiven even if they are as much as the foam of the sea."* (Sahih Muslim 597)

## Use QuranBookk's Digital Tasbih

Counting dhikr manually is the sunnah way — using fingers is recommended, as the Prophet ﷺ did. But in situations where you cannot count manually (driving, walking, working), a digital counter helps maintain consistency without losing count. QuranBookk's Digital Tasbih is beautifully designed — tap to count, set targets, track your daily dhikr with complete ease.

👉 [Use the Digital Tasbih on QuranBookk](/digital-tasbih)

## Building a Daily Dhikr Practice

<Callout type="tip">
The Prophet ﷺ's principle: *"The most beloved deeds to Allah are those done consistently, even if small."* (Sahih al-Bukhari 6465) A modest daily dhikr practice maintained for years is worth far more than an intense burst followed by months of nothing.
</Callout>

**After Fajr**: Morning adhkar (from *Hisnul Muslim* — Fortress of the Muslim), 33 tasbih/tahmid/takbir

**During commute or work**: La ilaha illallah, Subhan Allahi wa bihamdihi, Astaghfirullah

**After each prayer**: 33×3 + 1 = 100 phrases

**Before sleep**: Evening adhkar, Ayat al-Kursi, last two verses of Surah Al-Baqarah

**Whenever stressed**: La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah, Hasbunallahu wa ni'mal wakil

The entire set takes approximately 15–20 minutes per day. Yet the Prophet ﷺ described the reward of these phrases as heavier on the Scale than mountains.

## The Dhikr That Protects Your Heart

Ibn al-Qayyim writes that the heart is like a mirror. Every sin it commits dims it, every act of dhikr polishes it. A heart that remembers Allah constantly becomes a mirror that reflects divine light — it sees clearly, loves what is good, and recoils from what is harmful without even needing to reason it out.

The opposite is the *ghaflah* (heedlessness) — the state of forgetting Allah. The Quran describes this as the real death of the heart:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَلَا تَكُن كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ"
  translation="And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves."
  reference="Surah Al-Hashr 59:19"
/>

The person who forgets Allah loses the knowledge of their own purpose, their own worth, and their own direction. Dhikr is not a religious exercise — it is self-preservation.
